Israel was a unique people, set apart from other nations. See the benefit of publicly reading the word of God; when it is properly attended to, it reveals sin and duty, good and evil, and shows where we have gone wrong. We are helped when we are moved to separate ourselves from evil. Those who want to drive out sin from their hearts, which are living temples, must remove everything that supports it, and eliminate all things that feed and fuel lust; this is truly mortifying it. When sin is removed from the heart through repentance, let the blood of Christ be applied by faith, then let it be filled with the graces of God’s Spirit for every good work.

If a person in authority will not prevent others from setting an evil example, that person should not shelter anyone from deserved blame and punishment. The Levites had been wronged; their portions had not been given to them. They had gone to find ways to support themselves and their families, because their profession did not provide enough income. A lack of adequate financial support leads to a poor ministry. The work is neglected because the workers are. Nehemiah blamed the rulers for this situation. Both ministers and people who abandon religion and its services, and magistrates who do not do what they can to keep them on track, will have much to answer for. He quickly restored the Levites to their positions and ensured that they received just payment. Nehemiah looked to God in every situation and entrusted himself and all his affairs to Him. It pleased him to think that he had been helpful in reviving and supporting religion in his country. He refers to God, not out of pride, but with a humble appeal concerning his honest intentions in what he had done. He prays, “Remember me;” not, “Reward me.” “Do not erase my good deeds;” not, “Publicize them,” or “Record them.” Yet he was rewarded, and his good deeds were recorded. God does more than we can ask.

Observing the Lord’s day is an important focus for those who want to promote true godliness. Religion never thrives when sabbaths are disregarded. It is no surprise that there was a general decline in religion and a corruption of morals among the Jews, when they abandoned the sanctuary and desecrated the sabbath. Those who disregard the sabbath do not realize the harm they cause. We must answer for the sins others commit because of our example. Nehemiah considered it an evil thing, because it comes from contempt for God and our own souls. He shows that breaking the Sabbath was one of the sins for which God brought judgments upon them; and if they did not take warning but returned to those same sins, they should expect further judgments. The courage, zeal, and prudence of Nehemiah in this matter are recorded for us to follow suit; and we have reason to believe that the remedy he provided was lasting. He felt and confessed himself a sinner, who could demand nothing from God as justice when he cried out to him for mercy.

If either parent is ungodly, a child’s natural tendencies will lead them to resemble that parent; which is a strong reason why Christians should not be unequally yoked. In the education of children, great care should be taken about controlling their speech; they should not learn the language of Ashdod, no impious or impure talk, no corrupt communication. Nehemiah showed the harm in these marriages. Some, who were more stubborn than others, he struck, that is, ordered them to be beaten by officers according to the law. Here are Nehemiah’s prayers on this occasion: He prays, “Remember them, O my God.” Lord, convince and convert them; remind them of what they should be and do. The best services done for others have been forgotten by those who benefited from them, so Nehemiah refers himself to God, to receive compensation. This may well be the summary of our requests; we need no more to make us happy than this: “Remember me, O my God.” We may humbly hope that the Lord will remember us and our services, even though after lives of tireless activity and usefulness, we will still see cause to abhor ourselves and repent in dust and ashes, and cry out with Nehemiah, “Spare me, O my God, according to your great mercy.”
